Homework 1

Author: Matthew J. Crossley

Last update: 23 February, 2024

Learning objectives

This homework will assess the following:

  • Your ability to use R at a basic level.

  • Your ability to use built-in R help documents.

  • Your ability to wrangle data at a basic level in R using the data.table package.

  • Your ability to interact with data.table objects to report simple descriptive statistics.

  • Your ability to use data.table objects and ggplot to illustrate basic trends in data.

Instructions

  • Write a .Rmd script that replicates each ggplot figure and data.table output shown below exactly. When you are finished, knit your .Rmd to a .pdf file and submit it through ilearn.

Marking

  • 20% of your overall mark for this assessment will be determined from whether or not your code runs without error. If it does, then the full 20% will be given. If it does not, then you will lose this 20%. No partial credit will be given here.

  • 40% of your overall mark for this assessment will be determined from whether or not you replicate the ggplot figures exactly as shown below. If you get close but are not exact, then you will be awarded half of the total marks for that problem. All ggplot figures are equally weighted with respect to marks.

  • 40% of your overall mark for this assessment will be determined from whether or not you replicate the data.table output exactly as shown below. If you get close but are not exact, then you will be awarded half of the total marks for that problem. All data.table output is weighted equally with respect to marks.

0.

You will need to load the data.table and ggplot2 libraries and remove any lingering variables from your R session memory using rm(list=ls()). This problem set uses the iris data set that is loaded automatically when you load the ggplot2 package. By default, it exists as a data.frame so you will need to convert it to a data.table with the line iris <- as.data.table(iris). At this stage, you should have the following lines:

library(data.table)
library(ggplot2)

rm(list=ls())

iris <- as.data.table(iris)

1.

Create a histogram of Sepal.Length for species “setosa” and “versicolor” only, with a bin width of 0.5. Then, calculate the mean and range of Sepal.Length for these two species.

##       Species mean_Sepal.Length range_Sepal.Length
##        <fctr>             <num>              <num>
## 1:     setosa             5.006                1.5
## 2: versicolor             5.936                2.1

2.

Create a bar plot showing the count of each species grouped by Petal.Width categories (<2, ≥2). Then, calculate the count of each species within these Petal.Width categories.

##    Petal.Width.cat    Species     N
##             <char>     <fctr> <int>
## 1:              <2     setosa    50
## 2:              <2 versicolor    50
## 3:             >=2  virginica    29
## 4:              <2  virginica    21

3.

Create a boxplot of Sepal.Width by Species for species “setosa” and “virginica” only, colored by Species. Then, calculate the median and range of Sepal.Width for these species grouped by `

Petal.Length` categories (<4, ≥4).

##      Species Petal.Length.cat median_Sepal.Width range_Sepal.Width
##       <fctr>           <char>              <num>             <num>
## 1:    setosa               <4                3.4               2.1
## 2: virginica              >=4                3.0               1.6

4.

Plot Petal.Length vs Petal.Width for all species, colored by Species.
